briefs.title: Burnaby shelter at 3020 Gilmore must submit Good Neighbour and Operations Plans before opening
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has ruled that BC Housing and the Lookout Society must hand in a Good Neighbour Plan (a written commitment on how the shelter will work with the surrounding community) and an Operational Management Plan (a document outlining day-to-day running of the facility) before the city will approve occupancy of the new diversion shelter at 3020 Gilmore. Council also recommended that BC Housing and the Lookout Society hold broader public re-engagement before moving forward.
briefs.action_required: If your business is near 3020 Gilmore, watch for public re-engagement sessions and participate to raise any concerns.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Professional Services

briefs.title: Burnaby rezones 8265–8303 10th Avenue under updated zoning bylaw
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has given final approval to Bylaw No. 14780, which rezones (officially changes the permitted land use) for the properties at 8265 to 8303 10th Avenue. This means the allowed uses and development rules for these specific lots have now legally changed.

briefs.title: Burnaby updates its rules for how Council meetings are run
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council passed all three readings of an amendment to its Procedure Bylaw 2024, updating the formal rules that govern how Council meetings are conducted. This is an internal administrative change and does not directly impose new obligations on businesses.

briefs.title: Burnaby plumbing permit fees updated — costs change for construction projects requiring plumbing work
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has officially passed an amendment to its Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw, changing the fees charged for plumbing permits. If you plan to do any construction, renovation, or plumbing work in Burnaby that requires a permit, the cost of that permit has changed.
briefs.action_required: Check the updated Burnaby plumbing permit fee schedule before budgeting your next construction or renovation project.

briefs.title: Burnaby Bear Spray Bylaw Amendment Passes All Three Readings
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has passed all three readings of an amendment to its Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009, adding new rules around bear spray. This means bear spray is now subject to local bylaw enforcement in Burnaby, and businesses that sell or handle it may face new obligations.
briefs.action_required: Check with the City of Burnaby to confirm if your business is affected by the new bear spray rules.

briefs.title: Burnaby now regulates bear spray sales under its Business Licence Bylaw
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council passed all three readings of Bylaw #14798, which adds bear spray sales to the rules businesses must follow under the Burnaby Business Licence Bylaw 2017. This means selling bear spray in Burnaby is now a regulated activity, and businesses that sell it may need to meet new licensing conditions.
briefs.action_required: Check with the City of Burnaby if your business sells bear spray to confirm any new licence conditions.

briefs.title: Burnaby North Road BIA Bylaw 2026 approved — what local businesses need to know
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council passed all three readings of the Burnaby Business Improvement Area (North Road) Bylaw 2026. This formally renews or establishes the North Road BIA (a Business Improvement Area — a defined zone where local businesses collectively fund neighbourhood improvements and promotions) for 2026.
briefs.action_required: Check if your business is within the North Road BIA zone, as you may owe a BIA levy on your property tax bill.

briefs.title: Burnaby Rezones 7305 & 7317 Buller Ave — First and Second Reading Passed
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council passed the first and second readings of Bylaw #14794, which proposes to rezone (change the permitted land use) for two properties at 7305 and 7317 Buller Avenue. The bylaw still needs further readings and approval before the rezoning is finalized.
briefs.action_required: If you own, lease, or plan to develop property at 7305 or 7317 Buller Ave, monitor upcoming Council meetings for final approval.

briefs.title: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw text amendment passes first and second reading
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has advanced Bylaw No. 14792, a text amendment (a written change to the rules) to the Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965. The bylaw has passed its first and second readings, meaning it is moving through the approval process but has not yet become law.
briefs.action_required: Monitor Burnaby Council agendas for the third reading and final adoption of Bylaw No. 14792.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

briefs.title: Stoney Creek watershed development approvals may be paused pending staff review
briefs.summary: City council has sent a request from the Stoney Creek Environment Committee to staff for a formal report. The committee asked the city to create new protections for the Stoney Creek watershed (the land and waterways that drain into Stoney Creek) and to temporarily stop approving new development in that area until those protections are in place.
briefs.action_required: Monitor city staff report outcomes if you own or plan to develop land near the Stoney Creek watershed.

briefs.title: Stoney Creek construction impact rules under feasibility review — changes may follow
briefs.summary: City council has sent recommendations from the Environment Committee about reducing the impact of construction on Stoney Creek to staff for a feasibility review. Staff will study the proposed measures and report back before any new rules are put in place.
briefs.action_required: Monitor city staff reports for new construction rules near Stoney Creek that may affect your projects.

briefs.title: Council approves 2026 active transportation education and promotion initiatives
briefs.summary: City council has approved a set of education and promotion initiatives for active transportation (walking, cycling, and other non-motorized travel) for 2026, based on a Transportation Committee report from February 11, 2026. These programs are intended to encourage residents and commuters to use active travel options more often.

briefs.title: 49-Storey Mixed-Use Tower at Springer Avenue Clears First Step Toward Approval
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has approved a rezoning bylaw (a rule that changes how land can be used) for a 49-storey residential and retail tower at 2209 and 2225 Springer Avenue to move to First and Second Reading. The approval also includes a Housing Agreement and in-principle agreement to sell City-owned land at the site.
briefs.action_required: Monitor future public hearings for this rezoning if your business may be affected by the development.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

briefs.title: Blake Village rezoning at 6800 Lougheed Highway moves to first reading — height and density rules updated
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council has approved a rezoning bylaw (a rule that changes how land can be used or built on) for 6800 Lougheed Highway to move forward to its first and second formal readings. The update changes the allowed building height, massing (how large and bulky a building can be), and open space requirements for the Blake Village Master Plan site, bringing it in line with the city's long-term planning guide, OCP 2050.
briefs.action_required: Monitor Burnaby Council meeting agendas for the public hearing date on this rezoning bylaw.

briefs.title: 20-Storey Rental Tower at 5058 Imperial Street Replaces Approved 6-Storey Plan
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council has approved moving forward with a rezoning bylaw (a rule that changes how land can be used) for a 20-storey mixed-use market rental building at 5058 Imperial Street. This replaces a previously approved 6-storey plan for the same site and will now advance to First and Second Reading — the early formal steps before a bylaw becomes law.
briefs.action_required: If your business operates near 5058 Imperial Street, monitor upcoming public hearings for this rezoning.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

briefs.title: City-owned EV charging stations switch to energy-based fees under new bylaw amendments
briefs.summary: City council has approved a new way to charge drivers for using public electric vehicle (EV) charging stations — instead of paying by time, users will now pay based on the amount of energy they use (measured in kilowatt-hours). Several city bylaws, including the Parking Meter and EV Charging Meter Bylaw, have been updated to reflect this change.
briefs.action_required: If your business uses or manages City-owned EV chargers, review the new energy-based fee structure to update your cost estimates.

briefs.title: On-street public pay parking fees are going up — costs rise for customers and deliveries
briefs.summary: City council has approved an increase to on-street public pay parking rates, as outlined in the Financial Management Committee report from February 17, 2026. Businesses that rely on street parking for customers, deliveries, or staff should expect higher parking costs in their area.
briefs.action_required: Review how higher street parking costs may affect customer visits, delivery logistics, or staff commuting.

briefs.title: Heritage Alteration Permit granted for chimney work at 6490 Deer Lake Avenue, Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council has approved a Heritage Alteration Permit — a special permission required before making changes to a protected historic building — allowing chimney alterations at the W.J. Mathers House 'Altnadene' at 6490 Deer Lake Avenue. This means the proposed chimney work on this heritage-designated property is now officially authorized to proceed.

briefs.title: City renews $670,000 security contract with Guardteck for one more year
briefs.summary: City council approved a one-year renewal of its security services contract with Guardteck Security Corp, covering security officers at city-owned locations and special events. The total estimated value of the renewed contract is $670,000 including taxes.

briefs.title: City renews $162,400 portable toilet contract with Jiffy John Rentals
briefs.summary: City council approved a contract renewal with Jiffy John Rentals Ltd. to supply portable toilets at public sites, with a total estimated cost of $162,400 including taxes. This is a government procurement decision and does not directly change rules or requirements for private businesses.

briefs.title: Up to 84 outdoor events approved at Burnaby parks and stadium for 2026 season
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has approved noise bylaw exemptions for up to 84 special outdoor events at Deer Lake Park, Central Park, and Swangard Stadium. These events can take place between March 15 and November 1, 2026.
briefs.action_required: If you plan to host or vend at events at these venues in 2026, confirm your event falls within the approved window.

key_dates.label: Burnaby outdoor event season opens — noise bylaw exemptions take effect
Burnaby outdoor event season closes — noise bylaw exemption period ends

briefs.title: Burnaby Council approves Option B design for Landmark Burnaby Sign
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has approved the Option B design for the Landmark Burnaby Sign, directing staff to move forward with the project as described in the February 24, 2026 report. This is a civic identity initiative and does not introduce new rules or compliance requirements for businesses.

briefs.title: Burnaby freezes $63,005 FCM membership dues until city gets Big City Mayors' Caucus seat
briefs.summary: Burnaby's City Council has paused payment of its 2026–2027 membership dues to the Federation of Canadian Municipalities (FCM) — a national organization that represents cities and towns — worth $63,005.33. The payment is on hold until Burnaby is given a seat on the Big City Mayors' Caucus, a group within the FCM that gives larger cities a direct voice on national issues.

briefs.title: Commercial fleet parking permit renewed at 7000 Lougheed Highway
briefs.summary: Council approved the renewal of a Temporary Use Permit (a short-term permission to use land in a way not normally allowed under zoning rules) for 7000 Lougheed Highway. The permit allows commercial fleet vehicle parking and rentals to continue operating at that location.
briefs.action_required: If you operate at or near 7000 Lougheed Highway, confirm your activities align with the renewed permit conditions.

briefs.title: Antenna variance application at 9001 University Crescent sent back for further staff review
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council has sent a Development Variance Permit application (a request to bend standard building rules) back to city staff for more review. The application was seeking approval to install antenna equipment on a building at 9001 University Crescent that exceeds the normal limits for height and how much of the building face it can cover.

briefs.title: Antenna variance application at 8888 University Drive sent back for further staff review
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has sent a Development Variance Permit application (a request to bend standard building rules) back to staff for more review. The application sought permission to exceed normal limits on how much antenna equipment can cover a building's face and how tall that equipment can be at 8888 University Drive.

briefs.title: Burnaby grants utility access easement over 3860 Sperling Avenue for Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council approved a Statutory Right-of-Way (a legal permission allowing utility companies to access and use a strip of privately or publicly owned land) over part of 3860 Sperling Avenue. This allows third-party utility providers to run services to the Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre development.

briefs.title: Construction permits paused in Lower Westridge pending natural hazard safety assessment
briefs.summary: City council has launched a city-wide Natural Hazard Development Permit Area program to identify land that may be unsafe to build on due to risks like landslides or flooding. Council also approved $120,000 to assess hazards in the Lower Westridge neighbourhood and asked the Province to temporarily pause new construction permits there until the assessment is done.
briefs.action_required: If you plan to build or develop in Lower Westridge, check with the city before applying for any construction permits.

briefs.title: Burnaby now requires a special business licence condition to sell bear spray at retail
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has amended its Business Licence Bylaw and Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw to regulate the retail sale of bear spray. Retailers in Burnaby who sell bear spray must now meet new rules tied to their business licence (the permit that allows them to legally operate), and can face bylaw fines if they don't comply.
briefs.action_required: Check with the City of Burnaby to confirm what licence conditions apply to your bear spray sales.

briefs.title: Burnaby North Road BIA renewed for 2026–2031 with new bylaw coming
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has approved a five-year renewal of the North Road Business Improvement Area (BIA — a defined zone where local businesses collectively fund area improvements) for 2026–2031. The City Solicitor has been directed to draft the new Business Improvement Area Bylaw 2026, which will formalize the renewal.
briefs.action_required: If your business is on North Road in Burnaby, watch for the new BIA bylaw and any updated levy (fee) notices.

briefs.title: Burnaby plumbing permit fees updated — check new costs before your next project
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has approved small changes to the fees charged for plumbing permits under the Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw. If you plan to do any plumbing work that requires a permit, the cost to get that permit may be different than before.
briefs.action_required: Check Burnaby's updated plumbing permit fee schedule before applying for your next permit.

briefs.title: City will now remove outdated land-use restrictions from private properties on request
briefs.summary: The City has approved the removal of old restrictive covenants (legal rules written into a property's title that limit how the land can be used) that it previously placed on private properties. Property owners can now ask the City Solicitor to officially remove these outdated restrictions from their title.
briefs.action_required: If your property has a City-imposed restrictive covenant, contact the City Solicitor's office to request its removal.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail
